title: Reminiscence and Validation Therapy
subtitle: The nurse uses reminiscence (recalling past memories) and validation (accepting the person's feelings and reality) — helpful for older adults and dementia.

patient.name: Reminiscence / Validation
patient.admitDate: The nurse uses reminiscence and validation therapy.

nursesNote: Reminiscence therapy encourages a person to recall and share past memories (life review) and can lift mood, strengthen identity, and build connection, especially in older adults; the nurse uses photos, music, familiar objects, and open-ended prompts. Validation therapy is used with people who have dementia: instead of arguing, correcting, or forcing reality (reality orientation), the nurse acknowledges the person's feelings and their experienced reality and responds to the emotion behind the words, which reduces agitation and distress. Reality orientation is more helpful in delirium or early confusion but can increase distress in advanced dementia. The nurse uses a calm, patient, empathetic, nonjudgmental approach and individualizes the technique using the person's life history.
providerOrders: Use reminiscence to encourage sharing past memories
Use photos, music, and open-ended prompts
Use validation: acknowledge feelings and the person's reality
Do not argue with or force reality in advanced dementia
Respond to the emotion behind the words

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order using reminiscence with an older adult

opts.text: Create a calm, comfortable setting
Use photos, music, or objects as prompts
Ask open-ended questions about the past
Listen and validate feelings
Document the response
Looking through old photos together
Acknowledging a confused patient's fear
Asking about a favorite past job
Going along with the emotion behind their words
Playing music from their youth
Not arguing when they ask for a late parent

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient with dementia anxiously asks for her long-deceased mother

opts.text: Stay calm and approach gently
Acknowledge her feelings
Avoid bluntly saying her mother died
Ask about her mother to reminisce
Document the approach that helped
Acknowledging the patient's feelings
Repeatedly insisting her mother is dead
Using photos to prompt happy memories
Arguing to force the patient into reality
Responding to the emotion behind the words
Dismissing the patient's concern
Using a calm, patient approach
Reality orientation that agitates advanced dementia
